actions.thought: Starting the tournament with Js 3d on the button (small blind). While I have position post-flop, J3o is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity. At 100BB deep, there is no reason to risk chips or get involved in a potentially tricky pot with such a bottom-tier range hand against an opponent labeled as 'Pro'. I will fold and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the Button/Small Blind position with 94 offsuit, which is a very weak hand (bottom 15% of hands). While I have position post-flop in heads-up play, 94o lacks playability and equity against a standard defending range. At 100bb deep, there is no pressure to play garbage hands. The standard GTO play here is to fold.

Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand in a heads-up match, particularly from the button. I want to play this hand aggressively to seize control of the pot and capitalize on its high value relative to a random range. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate to build the pot while maintaining flexibility.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6d 3d facing a 3x raise (300 total) from the Button. While the Button's range is likely wide in heads-up play, my hand is weak (low suited two-gapper). I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 600, offering 33% pot odds. However, being out of position reduces my ability to realize equity significantly. Against a larger-than-minimum raise size, defending with the bottom of my suited range is generally -EV due to the positional disadvantage and lack of high-card strength. Folding is the prudent choice early in the match.

In heads-up play, K8o is a respectable hand, holding roughly 56% equity against a random hand. Being on the button allows me to play in position post-flop. I should not fold this hand. A min-raise to 200 serves to steal the blind or build a pot where I have the initiative and position. It balances my range effectively at 100bb deep.

I hold King-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 200, which is a standard minimum raise. KJo is a strong hand in heads-up play and easily warrants a call to see a flop. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the flop develops, as KJo plays well against a wide button-opening range.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a very dry board (J-7-3 rainbow). This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot. As the Big Blind, standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see if they will continuation bet. Donk-betting here would likely fold out their bluffs and only get called by better or similar hands. I will check and plan to call a bet or see a free turn card.

I hold K8o on a very dry J-7-3 rainbow board. While a continuation bet is standard in heads-up play on this texture to fold out air, my hand has marginal showdown value (King-high) that beats the hands I would fold out (Queen-high, Ten-high), but is behind the hands that will call me (any Pair, Ace-high). By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised, and can realize my equity by seeing a free turn card. This also strengthens my checking range, making me harder to exploit.

I have top pair with a King kicker. Since the opponent checked back the flop on a dry board, their range is likely capped, and it's highly probable that I currently hold the best hand. I will lead out with a bet of 300 (75% of the pot) to get value from weaker hands like middle pairs, pocket pairs (88-TT), or perhaps an Ace-high that decides to stick around.

I hold King-high (K8o) on a board of J-7-3-4. After I checked back the flop, my opponent has led out for 300 into a pot of 400 (75% pot) on the turn. This 'probe' bet often indicates a pair (like a 4, 7, or 3), a draw (hearts or straight), or a slow-played Jack. While I am ahead of pure air or missed draws, my hand is too weak to call a large bet with only King-high and no significant draw of my own. I have plenty of better hands in my range to defend here (pairs, ace-highs, draws). The pot odds (requiring ~30% equity) do not justify a call given the likelihood that I am behind or will face a river bet.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 6d 5s. This is a speculative hand that plays reasonably well in heads-up situations due to its connectivity. Based on previous hands, Pro has shown a willingness to fold to aggression. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and attempt to win the blinds immediately or play a controlled pot in position.

In a heads-up match, King-Six offsuit is a defensible hand from the Big Blind against a Button raise. The opponent has raised to 300 (3BB), requiring a call of 200 into a total pot of 600, offering 2:1 pot odds (33% equity required). Against a typical wide heads-up opening range (often 70%+ of hands), K6o has approximately 50% equity. While being out of position makes realizing equity harder, folding a King pre-flop to a single raise is generally too tight and exploitable. Therefore, calling to see a flop is the standard play.

The board is 4s 4h Qs. I hold K6o. In a heads-up pot, the pre-flop raiser (Button) has a range advantage on a Queen-high board, but the paired 4s mean it is hard for anyone to hit this hard. I have King-high, which has some showdown value. Leading out (donk betting) is generally poor strategy here as it folds out hands I beat and builds a pot against hands that beat me. The standard play is to check to the aggressor to see if they continue or give a free card.

The flop is 4s 4h Qs. As the pre-flop aggressor, I represent a strong range that includes many Queens and pocket pairs. Pro checked to me, which is a standard defensive play. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) to take advantage of my range lead and likely fold out his air or marginal hands. While I only have 6-high, the board texture favors the button raiser in this scenario.

The opponent c-bets 300 (half-pot) into a pot of 600 on a [4s 4h Qs] board. I hold King-Six offsuit, giving me King-high with no flush or straight draws. While heads-up ranges are wide, and K-high technically beats total air, I am out of position and the board texture (Queen high, flush draw present) connects reasonably well with a button opening range (Qx, Ax, Spades). The pot odds are 3:1 (25%), but realizing equity with K-high out of position against a half-pot bet is difficult. Without a pair or a draw, calling is a 'float' with negative expected value against a balanced range. Preserving chips for better spots is the disciplined play.

I am holding 8c 2c in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand (bottom 15-20% of hands) even in a heads-up context. While I have position post-flop, the hand suffers from poor playability and reverse implied odds. Standard Heads-Up strategy suggests folding this hand most of the time, especially 100BB deep where reverse implied odds matter more. Conserving the 0.5 BB is better than entering a pot with a hand that rarely connects well.

I have been dealt Pocket Queens, which is a premier starting hand in heads-up play. My goal is to extract value and build a pot. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that is consistent with my previous play style in this session, as seen in Hands #3 and #5. I want to encourage a call from a wider range of hands while maintaining pressure.

I am in the Big Blind holding 3d 6h, which is a statistically very weak hand (bottom 10% of hands). My opponent on the Button (SB) has raised to 300 chips (3BB). In heads-up play, the BB defends widely, but usually against min-raises (2BB). Against a 3x raise, my pot odds are 200 to call into a total pot of 600 (33% equity required). 63o has poor playability and is unlikely to realize its raw equity out of position. Given the larger sizing and the weakness of my hand, folding is the standard and correct play.

In Heads-Up play, being on the Button (SB) allows me to act last post-flop. K6s is a strong starting hand in this format (top 30-40% of hands). My opponent has been aggressive from the SB, but I haven't seen them 3-bet aggressively from the BB yet. A min-raise to 200 is a standard play to steal the blind or build a pot in position with a playable hand. Folding is incorrect due to hand strength, and limping is passive compared to seizing the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qd2d. The Button has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. I only need to call 100 more chips into a pot that will be 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 2d is a weak kicker, Queen-high suited has enough playability to defend against a wide button opening range. Folding would be too tight given the price, and raising is unnecessary given my positional disadvantage.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen, but the board is very dangerous with three clubs (monotone) and connected cards (7 and 8). Since I am out of position and my kicker is the lowest possible (2), I will check to see how the opponent acts. This allows me to control the pot size and avoid overcommitting against a potential flush, set, or better Queen.

I hold King-high (Ks 6s) on a monotone club board (7c Qc 8c). This board texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many suited hands and connectors. I possess no clubs, meaning I have no immediate draws to a flush, and my hand has very little equity if called. Betting as a bluff is ineffective here because the opponent will likely continue with any club, pair, or straight draw. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity with King-high, and see a free turn card to potentially improve to a pair.

The board has now put out four clubs, meaning any single club in my opponent's hand beats my two pair. While the deuce improved my hand to two pair, its primary impact is the fourth club on the board. Since I am out of position and the board is extremely coordinated, I will check to see how the opponent reacts. Betting here would likely only be called by a club, and I want to reach a cheap showdown if possible.

The board is now a four-flush with [7c Qc 8c 2c]. I hold [Ks 6s] and do not have a club. This means I am playing the board. If the opponent holds any club, they have a flush and beat me. If they do not hold a club, we will likely split the pot. Betting here has very little utility: better hands (any club) will never fold, and hands that I chop with (no club) will likely call or are getting a free ride to a split pot anyway. Bluffing into a four-flush board without a blocker is -EV. The best play is to check back, minimize loss against a flush, and hope to get to showdown for a chop if the opponent also lacks a club.

The board has five clubs, meaning a Queen-high flush is available to both players. Since I do not hold a club in my hand that is higher than the lowest club on the board (the 2c or 6c), I am currently 'playing the board'. I can only win if the opponent also plays the board (resulting in a split pot) or lose if the opponent holds a club higher than the 6c (specifically the 9c, Tc, Jc, Kc, or Ac). There is no value in betting because I cannot beat any hand and the opponent will not fold a hand that is splitting with me. I will check and hope to split the pot.

The board shows [7c Qc 8c 2c 6c], which is a five-card flush. Since I do not hold a club (my hand is Ks 6s), I am playing the board (Queen-high flush). If the opponent holds any club, they have a higher flush and win. If they do not hold a club, we split the pot. Betting here achieves nothing: hands with a club will not fold, and hands without a club will likely call for the split. Checking back ensures I do not lose additional chips to a hidden flush and allows me to realize the split pot if the opponent also lacks a club.

Hand 94o is a weak hand even in a heads-up situation. While playing a wide range from the button is standard, 94o sits near the bottom of that range and doesn't play well post-flop. Given that I have maintained a slight lead and have been aggressive in previous hands (raising in hands 3, 5, and 7), I can afford to fold this junk hand and wait for a better spot rather than over-committing with air.

In a heads-up scenario, A3o is a statistically strong hand to play from the button (Small Blind). It has blockers to Aces and often leads against random holdings. My strategy is to raise to 200 (min-raise) to apply pressure, define ranges, and utilize my positional advantage post-flop. If the opponent 3-bets, I can comfortably fold, but if they call, I am in a good spot to c-bet or check back depending on the board texture.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-6 offsuit. The opponent in the Small Blind/Button has raised to 200. This is a 2x raise, and I only need to call 100 more chips into a pot that will be 400. This gives me 3:1 pot odds, which is more than enough to justify defending my blind with a connected hand in a heads-up match, despite being out of position for the rest of the hand.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) on a 9-5-10 rainbow board. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here doesn't make much sense as this board texture doesn't significantly favor my range over the button's range.

I hold Ac 3s on a flop of 9s 5d Th. This is a rainbow board that connects fairly well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs of T, 9, 5, straight draws like J8, QJ, 76). My Ace-high has some showdown value, but I have no draws and very little equity if called. Betting would likely only fold out worse hands (like K-high) and isolate me against better hands or strong draws. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, potentially hit an Ace on the turn, or get to a cheap showdown without bloating the pot with a marginal holding.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). The board paired on the turn with a second 5. Pro checked back on the flop, which often indicates a capped range or a medium-strength hand. I will check here to see Pro's reaction. If Pro checks back, I get a free river to try and hit my straight. If Pro bets, I can decide whether to call based on the pot odds and the likelihood of my 8 being the winning card.

The turn brings a second 5 (9s 5d Th 5s). The action went check-check on the flop. Flash checks again on the turn. My hand (Ac 3s) is currently Ace-high. The board pairing the 5 doesn't significantly change the strength of my hand relative to the opponent's range, although it makes trips possible for them (though less likely given the passive play). Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, and it is unlikely I get called by worse hands, while better hands (any pair) will call. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity, and try to get to a cheap showdown where Ace-high often wins in heads-up play, or hit an Ace on the river.

I have 7-high and no showdown value. The action has been checked through on the flop and the turn, which suggests that Pro likely has a weak hand or a medium-strength hand they are playing cautiously. While betting could potentially force a fold from some of Pro's range (like King-high or weak Ace-high), the pot is small and I have no real equity if called. Checking is the most disciplined play here to see a showdown for free or fold if Pro decides to bet, as I don't want to unnecessarily bleed chips early in the tournament with a total air hand.

I arrived at the river with Ace-high after checking back the flop and turn. The board reads 9s 5d Th 5s 4d. The opponent has checked to me three times, indicating significant weakness, likely missed draws or weak high-card hands. My Ace-high has decent showdown value in this spot. Betting would likely only fold out hands that I already beat (like King-high or Queen-high) and isolate me against pairs that would call. There is no value in betting, and turning an Ace into a bluff is unnecessary when it has a good chance of winning at showdown. Therefore, the optimal play is to check back and take the free showdown.

In heads-up play, any King-high hand is generally strong enough to open for a raise from the button. I have been consistent with 3x raises (300 total) which has successfully won the blinds or allowed me to take control of the pot post-flop in previous hands. I will continue this strategy with K2o.

I hold Queen-Jack suited (Qc Jc) in the Big Blind, facing a 3x open (300) from the Button. This is a premium defending hand that plays well post-flop due to its flush and straight potential. While a 3-bet is an option, calling is the standard play to keep the opponent's range wide and control the pot size while out of position. The price of 200 chips to call into a pot of 400 offers good odds.

I hold QJs on a 6s-7c-Td board. I defended my Big Blind against a button raise. The flop texture is somewhat connected but missed my hand directly, leaving me with two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is not advisable here as my hand has limited equity and does not block value hands well. I will check and evaluate the opponent's action.

The flop of 6-7-T is somewhat coordinated and hits a big blind's calling range, but as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain pressure. Pro checked to me, which often indicates weakness or a marginal hand. A continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard play here to take down the pot immediately or narrow down his range. I have King-high, which has some showdown value but mostly relies on fold equity on this texture.

I hold QcJc on a 6s-7c-Td board. While I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw, this board texture (low, connected) favors the opponent's button opening range significantly (hitting sets, two pairs, and straights). Notably, in previous hands (Hands 4, 8, 10), this opponent has shown a tendency to check back the flop; their decision to bet here suggests they likely connected with the board or hold a strong draw. With only Queen-high, no immediate straight or flush draw, and being out of position against a range that likely has me beat or dominated, floating is -EV. The disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold 5h 6c on the Button (Small Blind). This is a weak off-suit connector. In heads-up play, while ranges are wide, 65o is near the bottom of playable hands. Given the opponent's tendency to defend their big blind and play aggressively post-flop (as seen in Hands #4, #5, #11), opening with such a weak hand is likely -EV. Folding is the standard play here to preserve chips.

I have Ace-Seven suited on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium hand in this context, possessing both high card value and flush potential. I will put in a standard raise to 300 chips to build the pot and take control of the hand, maintaining my aggressive strategy from the button.

I am in the Big Blind with J8s (Jack-Eight of clubs) facing a 3x open raise to 300 from the Button. This is a standard Heads-Up defending situation. The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 200 to win a pot that will be 600, requiring 25% raw equity. J8s has approximately 40-45% equity against a typical wide button opening range. My hand plays reasonably well post-flop due to its flush and straight potential. Folding would be too tight, and while a 3-bet is an option, calling is the most standard and lower-variance line out of position against this sizing.

I am out of position on a [4d 7h Ts] board with Jc8c. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a 9) and a backdoor flush draw. The board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (lots of broadway/10x hands). Leading out (donk betting) is unlikely to accomplish much, as I will likely be called by better hands or raised. Checking is the standard line here to see how the opponent proceeds. If they check back, I realize equity for free. If they bet, I can evaluate based on sizing.

I have middle pair with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry board. Pro has checked to me, which is expected as the pre-flop caller. I will place a continuation bet of 300 chips, which is half the pot, to extract value from weaker hands like lower pairs or straight draws (56, 89) and to protect my hand against overcards that could peel on the turn.

I am facing a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600) on a 4d 7h Ts board. I hold Jc8c, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a 9), a backdoor flush draw, and one overcard (Jack) to the board. In previous hands (#5, #11), this opponent has taken the exact same line (raise pre, c-bet 300), and I folded both times. To avoid being exploited by high-frequency c-bets, I must defend here. The pot odds require roughly 25% equity. With my draws and overcard, along with implied odds if I hit the straight, calling is a positive expected value play compared to folding. Raising is an option but calling keeps the pot manageable out of position.

The turn brings the Ten of Hearts (Th), pairing the board (4d 7h Ts Th). I hold Jc8c, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and a Jack overcard to the lower pairs. The board pairing makes trips possible for anyone holding a Ten, which is well within the opponent's pre-flop raising range. Leading out (donk betting) here is not advisable as it folds out bluffs I beat and gets called by better hands. Checking is the standard play to the pre-flop aggressor who c-bet the flop. I want to see the river card as cheaply as possible to try and hit my straight. If the opponent checks back, I realize my equity for free.

The turn card paired the Ten, the highest card on the board. While I have two pair (Tens and Sevens with an Ace kicker), the board texture has become more dangerous as it's now possible for my opponent to have a Ten. Checking back on the turn allows me to control the size of the pot and realize my equity without risking a check-raise. This strategy also keeps my range protected and allows me to see a river card for free, potentially catching bluffs from missed draws like 89 or 56 on the final street.

I hold Jc8c on a board of 4d 7h Ts Th Kd. I missed my straight draw and now hold a pair of Tens (playing the board) with a King, Jack, Seven kicker (effectively Jack-high relative to the board's community cards). The opponent checked back the turn, which often indicates marginal showdown value or a draw. The River King is a scare card that favors the opponent's range more than mine. I currently beat missed straight draws (like 89, 56) due to my Jack kicker. Betting would likely only fold out hands I already beat and get called by hands that beat me (Kx, Ax, any pocket pair). Checking allows me to try and get to showdown cheaply to see if my Jack-kicker holds up against a busted draw, or to fold if the opponent bets strongly representing strength.

On this river, the King is an overcard that potentially improves my opponent's range if they were calling with hands like K-J or K-Q on the flop. Given the board has paired the Tens and I have Sevens, my hand has decent showdown value but is likely not strong enough to bet for value. A bet here is unlikely to be called by a worse hand and could be met with a check-raise from a Ten or a King. Therefore, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and see the showdown in a controlled pot.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, King-Six offsuit (K6o) is a playable hand from the Button (SB), ranking well within the top 50% of hands. As the Button, I have the advantage of position post-flop. My opponent has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind, but K6o has reasonable equity and blocks King-high hands. Folding is too passive for this holding, and limping allows the opponent to see a flop for free with their entire range. A minimum raise to 200 applies pressure, gives me the initiative, and allows me to play the pot in position if called.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh 4c. The opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 200, which is a minimum raise. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, J4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given that I have a healthy chip lead and there are many hands left in the tournament, I will opt to fold this marginal hand rather than risk chips in a potentially difficult spot out of position.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit, which is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. My goal is to maintain pressure and build the pot. I have consistently raised to 300 from the button in previous hands, and I will continue that pattern here to represent strength and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ah4s facing a 3x open (300) from the Button. The pot odds require me to call 200 into a pot that will be 600, needing 33% equity. A4o has significantly more equity than 33% against a typical wide button opening range (often 50%+ of hands). While being out of position is a disadvantage, the price is too good to fold an Ace. 3-betting is an option as a bluff, but calling is the standard, lower-variance play to realize equity against an aggressive opponent.

I hold Ah4s on a 7s-6s-Jc board. I have Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw (one spade in hand, two on board). As the pre-flop caller out of position, it is standard to check to the aggressor. The board texture connects somewhat with a button opening range (Jacks, draws). Leading out (donk betting) is unlikely to be profitable as it folds out bluffs I beat and gets called by better hands. My plan is to check and evaluate the opponent's action; against a standard c-bet, I will likely have to fold given the weak nature of my holding and lack of immediate draws.

I have middle pair (sevens) with an Ace kicker on a semi-coordinated board of 7-6-J. After raising pre-flop and being called by Pro, I currently hold a strong hand for heads-up play. Pro has checked to me on the flop, providing an opportunity for a continuation bet to build the pot and protect my hand against various draws (straight and flush). A bet of 300 into the 600 pot is standard and serves to extract value from weaker hands or draws while maintaining control of the hand.

I hold Ah4s on a 7s 6s Jc board. I have Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw. My opponent, the pre-flop aggressor, has continuation bet 300 (half pot). While I have some equity against pure bluffs, the board texture is wet and connects well with many hands in the opponent's range (top pairs, straight draws). Calling out of position with just Ace-high and weak backdoor draws is generally -EV against a half-pot bet. I lack sufficient immediate equity or strong enough implied odds to float here. The prudent play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the Button (SB) with J4o, which is a very weak hand, typically in the bottom 20% of starting hands. While I have position, the hand has poor playability and is generally not profitable to open, especially against an opponent who has shown a willingness to defend their Big Blind. Folding is the standard play here to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Qc9c, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, especially suited. I have been playing aggressively and want to maintain that pressure on 'Pro', who has been folding quite often pre-flop. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard open that allows me to take control of the hand or potentially win the pot immediately if the opponent folds.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. My opponent 'Flash' has shown a pattern of frequent 3x opens and continuation bets. T2o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position. Although I am getting 2:1 pot odds, my equity realization against an aggressive opponent will be low. Defending here would likely result in bleeding chips long-term. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.

I hold AJo on the Button, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Looking at my history, I have been opening with a min-raise (200) consistently when entering the pot from the button (e.g., Hand #4, #8, #10, #14). Flash has often defended their Big Blind by calling these raises. I want to build the pot for value while keeping my opening size consistent to balance my range. Therefore, raising to 200 is the standard and optimal play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. The opponent has been consistently raising from the button to 200. I have a significant chip lead and want to maintain my aggression. A 3-bet to 600 serves to either take the pot down immediately or build value against a wider button opening range. Given the history where the opponent often folds to pressure or folds to continuation bets, this is an ideal spot to increase the stakes pre-flop.

I hold AJo on the Button. After opening to 200, I face a 3-bet to 600 from the Big Blind. This is the first pre-flop 3-bet I have encountered from Flash, suggesting strength or a polarized bluff. However, AJo is a premium hand in heads-up play, and I have position on the opponent. I need to call 400 into a pot of 800, offering 3:1 pot odds. I need approximately 25% equity to continue, which AJo easily has against even a tight 3-bet range. 4-betting could isolate me against a stronger range or force a fold to a shove, whereas calling allows me to play a pot in position with a strong hand against a wider range.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Eight offsuit and Pro called. The flop comes King-high (Kd 6c 5h), which is very favorable for a 3-betting range that includes many big pairs and AK/KQ. As the aggressor, I want to continue representing strength. A 1/3 pot continuation bet is effective on such a dry board to fold out underpairs or broadway cards that missed. Given that Pro has shown a tendency to fold to aggression in previous hands, this bet is likely to take the pot down immediately.

I hold AJo on the Button. After calling a pre-flop 3-bet to 600, the pot is 1200. The flop comes Kd-6c-5h, a very dry texture that connects well with a strong 3-betting range (typically AK, AA, KK, KQ). The opponent bets 400 (1/3 pot). This is the first time Flash has 3-bet pre-flop, suggesting a strong hand range (likely TT+, AQ+). Against this range on a King-high board, my equity is poor (often drawing to 3 outs or dead against AK/AA). Even though the bet is small offering 3:1 pot odds (requiring 20% equity), my equity against a value-heavy range is likely around 12-15%. Furthermore, I face reverse implied odds if an Ace hits (potentially losing to AK). Without a specific read that the opponent is 3-betting light, the disciplined play is to respect the strength shown and fold.

I am on the button with T3o, which is a very weak hand. While I have been successfully applying pressure and winning several pots pre-flop and with C-bets, T3o is near the bottom of my range and lacks connectivity or high-card value. Given my stack lead and the fact that I've been opening frequently, I'll choose to fold this hand to maintain a more credible range for my aggressive plays later.

I hold Jd 2h in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand (trash tier), typically in the bottom 20% of starting hands. In heads-up play, while ranges are wide, J2o is usually a fold, especially against an opponent who has shown aggression (3-betting and frequent raising). I previously folded J4o in the same position (Hand #16). Since I am out of position post-flop and the hand has poor playability and equity, preserving my stack of 7600 is preferable to bleeding chips or facing a raise. Folding is the most prudent action.

I am on the button with a suited hand. In a heads-up match, a 10-high suited hand is strong enough to open for a raise to maintain pressure and utilize my positional advantage. I have flopped trip Jacks with a King kicker (Jd As Js), a monster hand in heads-up poker. The opponent (Flash) has continuation bet 300 into a pot of 600. On the river, the board has completed a Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-10) with my Queen of hearts. I hold a Full House (Jacks full of Kings) on a board of Jd-As-Js-Tc-Kh. This is a monster hand, only losing to AJ, AA, KK, or TT.
